rationale: This is a canonical, textbook example of the Wolff-Kishner reduction. It uses a simple, acyclic, aliphatic ketone (3-pentanone) and reduces it to the corresponding alkane (n-pentane). The reagents, hydrazine (NN) and potassium hydroxide ([OH-].[K+]), are the classic reagents for this transformation. The structure is simple, RDKit-parseable, and clearly demonstrates the core C=O to CH2 conversion without any complicating functional groups.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple aliphatic ketone to avoid overlap with reactions involving aromatic rings (e.g., Friedel-Crafts acylation followed by reduction), which could be confused with the Clemmensen reduction. It avoids aldehydes which could undergo aldol or other base-catalyzed side reactions. The simple structure is distinct from ring contractions (Favorskii) or other named reactions involving hydrazones (e.g., Shapiro, Bamford-Stevens).
